title: The Wealth Bias in Elite University Admissions
content:
Admission to highly selective universities for talented students outside the traditional elite is much more restricted than it ought to be. Recent research on the 12 Ivy-Plus colleges in the U.S. shows that students from wealthy backgrounds are heavily overrepresented on these campuses. More than 15% of the student body comes from families in the top 1% of the U.S. income distribution, who earn over $600,000 per year. Data from other countries also bears this out; for instance, students from private high schools in the U.K. account for nearly half of those at Oxford and Cambridge. In Chile, students from elite private schools are 16 times as likely as the average student to enroll in the nation’s top universities.

Recruited athletes, who account for up to 15% of top American universities’ incoming students, also skew heavily toward high-income families. These families are uniquely able to provide the expensive coaching and support needed for students to excel in niche athletics. One might be able to justify these admission preferences if they helped universities find students with unusually high potential. However, comprehensive data show just the opposite: these admission advantages do not predict future success or intellectual contribution.

Legacy students, those with higher nonacademic ratings, and recruited athletes are no more likely to reach top income levels than comparable Ivy-Plus applicants without those advantages. In many cases, they are actually less likely to work at prestigious companies or pursue highly competitive graduate degrees. By contrast, traditional academic factors such as standardized test scores and high school grades remain highly predictive of postcollege outcomes. This suggests that elite universities are sacrificing academic excellence to maintain a wealth-biased admissions system.
